fuzzy_prd: We need to finish the black-box surface for our transparent build accelerator, not just the wire serialization layer. The tool should expose a deterministic adapter for the same scenarios our tests cover: command-line/help and invalid option handling, direct command execution with stdout/stderr/exit status passthrough, descendant process interception across common execution methods, environment-fixup behavior for missing or hostile environment variables, diagnostic stream passthrough for warnings/errors, cache-hit acceleration behavior across repeated builds, and the typed wire-message serialization format used internally by the accelerator. Keep the core accelerator/process/serialization logic separate from the JSON adapter, and preserve the exact stdout contracts in the public cases.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with op) -> stdout for operations cli, execute, intercept, env_fixup, diagnostics, accelerate, and serialize

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The wire frame's declared payload length must be padded to an 8-byte boundary. Both the declared length in the frame header and the actual bytes written must reflect this padding. The test contract checks `payload_padded_to_8=true` and `declared_length_matches_payload=true` — both must pass simultaneously.

If any required field is absent when building a message, the system must reject the build before producing any wire bytes. The error must be reported as `error=missing_required_field` with a `field=<name>` line naming the first missing required field. The required fields are: ri1 (required int), ri3 (required int), rs5 (required string), rs7 (required string). Optional fields are oi2, oi4, os6, os8, sa9, sa10.

Each optional field must expose a boolean presence flag in the decoded output. For optional integers (oi2, oi4) and optional strings (os6, os8), when absent the output must include `has_<fieldname>=false` with no value line emitted. When present, `has_<fieldname>=true` followed by `<fieldname>=<value>`. String arrays (sa9, sa10) that are unset or explicitly empty decode as `<name>_size=0` with no element lines.

The fixed schema has exactly 10 fields: ri1 (required int), oi2 (optional int), ri3 (required int), oi4 (optional int), rs5 (required string), os6 (optional string), rs7 (required string), os8 (optional string), sa9 (string array), sa10 (string array). The numeric suffix is part of the canonical field name used in both wire encoding and decoded output.

Every message carries an acknowledgement id (ack) that is separate from the payload fields. It is passed at message-build time and must round-trip through the wire frame, appearing as `ack_id=<value>` as the first line of the decoded output. The ack value can be 0 or any integer.

String arrays decode as a size line followed by indexed element lines: `<name>_size=<n>` then `<name>[0]=<val>`, `<name>[1]=<val>`, etc. An empty or absent array emits only `<name>_size=0`. Array elements preserve their exact string content including any leading/trailing characters.

An empty string supplied for a required string field (rs5 or rs7) is valid and must round-trip as an empty value: `rs5=` with nothing after the equals sign. This is distinct from an absent optional field — the field is present, just empty.

The decoded output must follow this exact line order: ack_id, declared_length_matches_payload, payload_padded_to_8, then fields in schema order: ri1, has_oi2 (+ oi2 if present), ri3, has_oi4 (+ oi4 if present), rs5, has_os6 (+ os6 if present), rs7, has_os8 (+ os8 if present), sa9_size (+ sa9 elements), sa10_size (+ sa10 elements).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'cli', 'execute', 'intercept', 'env_fixup', 'diagnostics', 'accelerate', 'serialize'. Each value maps to one feature.
The task covers all public op values: cli, execute, intercept, env_fixup, diagnostics, accelerate, and serialize. Serialization is only Feature 7 and must not be treated as the entire task.
The adapter reads one JSON object from stdin and routes by top-level op. Preserve each op's operation-specific input fields and exact stdout contract from public_test_cases.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 in the PRD: the compact, allocation-free typed wire-message layer described in features 7_1 (serialize all fields), 7_2 (optionals absent), and 7_3 (missing required fields), with test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature7_*.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: alignment requirements we talked about in the architecture review
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The wire frame payload must be padded to an 8-byte boundary; both `declared_length_matches_payload=true` and `payload_padded_to_8=true` must appear in decoded output, as specified in feature7_1_serialize_all_fields.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar vibe to the compatibility logic from that login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The separation-of-concerns constraint from the PRD Architecture section: the serialization layer must be decoupled from JSON/stdin/stdout; a thin execution adapter (the op=serialize handler) translates JSON input into calls to the core serializer
Error output format: `error=missing_required_field\nfield=<fieldname>\n` — no exceptions, no language-specific error types, matching the pattern used by feature7_3_serialize_missing_required.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — please check the existing adapter test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature7_*.json for the exact expected output format, and refer to the architecture constraints around separation of concerns for how the serializer should be structured internally.

feature1_1_cli_help.json.description: Front-end help output normalized to its argument-parsing surface: the usage banner is shown, the full set of advertised long options is reported in sorted order, the documented contract that the wrapped command's exit code is passed through is detected, and the front-end itself exits 0.

feature1_2_cli_invalid_option.json.description: When the front-end is given an unrecognized option letter, it reports the offending option in a normalized form, still prints the usage banner, and exits with the failure status 1.

feature2_execute_passthrough.json.description: A shell command run transparently under the accelerator produces exactly the stdout it would produce without it, and its exit status is propagated unchanged. Covers plain output, output filtered through a pipe, and a non-zero exit code.

feature3_1_intercept_direct.json.description: A program launched directly by the accelerator inherits the accelerator's interception library in its dynamic-preload environment, so the running child can observe that interception is active. The child also confirms it actually ran.

feature3_2_intercept_spawn_methods.json.description: Interception follows a descendant regardless of which standard process-creation mechanism the parent uses to spawn it. For each of fork-then-exec, pipe-open, spawn, and the C library command runner, the spawned child observes the interception library injected into its preload environment.

feature3_3_intercept_static_binary.json.description: A statically linked program (which cannot have any library injected into it) is still correctly run to completion when launched through each standard process-creation mechanism, and its own stdout passes straight through the accelerator unchanged.

cases.expected_output:
exit_status=0
stdout<<<
I am statically linked.
>>>

exit_status=0
stdout<<<
I am statically linked.
>>>

exit_status=0
stdout<<<
I am statically linked.
>>>

exit_status=0
stdout<<<
I am statically linked.
>>>

feature4_env_fixup.json.description: Even when a build command completely rebuilds its own environment and writes a deliberately malformed preload list (whose only valid entry is the accelerator's own interception library, surrounded by garbage entries and extra spaces), the interception library still reaches the child while every user-supplied variable is preserved verbatim. The dynamic loader's complaints about the garbage entries are normalized into neutral warning lines.

cases.expected_output:
exit_status=0
AAA=aaa
BBB=bbb
interceptor_present=true
preload_warning=BOGUS1.SO
preload_warning=BOGUS2.SO

feature5_diagnostics_passthrough.json.description: Standard library diagnostic streams produced by a wrapped program pass through the accelerator unchanged, and the normal program-termination path is preserved: a sequence of non-fatal warnings followed by a fatal one, an at-exit handler that still runs, and a failure exit status. The 'warn' variant reports each diagnostic with the errno-derived message; the 'error' variant uses the alternative diagnostic routine. Each diagnostic line is prefixed by a neutral fixed program token.

cases.expected_output:
exit_status=1
stderr<<<
prog: w1: No such file or directory
prog: w2: Permission denied
prog: e1: No such file or directory
atexit_handler
>>>

exit_status=1
stderr<<<
prog: e1: No such file or directory
prog: e2: Permission denied
prog: e3: No such file or directory
atexit_handler
>>>

feature6_accelerate_cache_hit.json.description: Compiling the same source file twice against a fresh cache demonstrates the core acceleration guarantee: the first compilation is a cache miss and is executed normally, while the second compilation of the identical input is served from cache instead of being re-executed. Both runs succeed.

input.source: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

cases.expected_output:
run=1
exit_status=0
served_from_cache=false
run=2
exit_status=0
served_from_cache=true

feature7_1_serialize_all_fields.json.description: A message carrying every kind of field (required and optional integers, required and optional strings, and two string arrays) is serialized over the wire frame and decoded back. The decoded message reproduces the acknowledgement id, confirms the frame's declared payload length matches the bytes written and is padded to an 8-byte boundary, and reproduces every scalar, string, presence flag, and array element exactly.

cases.expected_output:
ack_id=123
declared_length_matches_payload=true
payload_padded_to_8=true
ri1=42
has_oi2=true
oi2=100
ri3=-200
has_oi4=true
oi4=-5
rs5=foo
has_os6=true
os6=loremipsum
rs7=quux
has_os8=true
os8=opt
sa9_size=3
sa9[0]=item1
sa9[1]=item02
sa9[2]=item003
sa10_size=1
sa10[0]=x

feature7_2_serialize_optionals_absent.js.description: When only the required fields are provided, every optional integer and optional string decodes as absent (its presence flag is false and no value line is emitted), and unset string arrays decode as empty (size 0). An empty string supplied for a required string round-trips as an empty value, and an explicitly declared but empty string array also decodes as size 0.

cases.expected_output:
ack_id=0
declared_length_matches_payload=true
payload_padded_to_8=true
ri1=7
has_oi2=false
ri3=-7
has_oi4=false
rs5=hello
has_os6=false
rs7=world
has_os8=false
sa9_size=0
sa10_size=0

ack_id=99
declared_length_matches_payload=true
payload_padded_to_8=true
ri1=1
has_oi2=false
ri3=2
has_oi4=false
rs5=
has_os6=false
rs7=z
has_os8=false
sa9_size=0
sa10_size=0

feature7_3_serialize_missing_required.js.description: Building a message without all of its mandatory fields is rejected before any wire bytes are produced. The result is a neutral error category naming the first missing required field rather than a partial or corrupt message.
